# Topic Overview

- **Topic:** The Chernobyl accident in 1986 — causes and consequences.
- **Source context:** Lecture by **Per Hedemann Jensen**, Risø National Laboratory, delivered at the **Institute of Physics and Astronomy, University of Aarhus**, **30 November 1994**.
- **Accident date and time:** Early hours of **Saturday 26 April 1986**, around **01:24 Moscow time**.
- **Location:** **Chernobyl nuclear power plant**, Unit 4, USSR.
- **Main event:** Two explosions destroyed the Unit 4 reactor building, exposing the reactor core and releasing radioactive material into the atmosphere.
- **Main themes covered:**
  - Causes and initiating events.
  - Radionuclide release and accident management.
  - Radiation biology.
  - International Chernobyl Project.
  - Consequences inside and outside the USSR.
  - Radiation dose quantities and units.

# Main Concepts

## The Chernobyl Unit 4 accident

- At the time of the accident:
  - Nearly **200 employees** were working on Units 1, 2, 3 and the experiment at Unit 4.
  - A further **300 people** were working nearby on the construction of Units 5 and 6.
- At around **01:24 Moscow time**, two explosions:
  - Blew the roof off the Unit 4 reactor building.
  - Sent concrete, graphite and debris into the air.
  - Exposed the reactor core to the outside air.
  - Released uranium fuel, transuranics, fission products and noble gases.
- Smoke and fumes rose over **1 km** into the air.
- Heavy radioactive material fell near the site.
- Lighter radioactive particles formed a cloud that contaminated surfaces where it touched down.

## Reactor test and safety failures

- The planned test on **25–26 April 1986** was an electrical systems test before scheduled maintenance.
- Its purpose was to test whether turbine generators could support essential systems during a major station blackout.
- The test was treated as **purely electrotechnical**, meaning electrical rather than nuclear.
- Nuclear safety was given little emphasis.
- Proper authorizations were not obtained.
- Several safety systems or shutdown signals were blocked or switched off.

## RBMK reactor characteristics

- Chernobyl Unit 4 was an **RBMK** reactor.
- RBMK stands for **Reaktor Bolshoy Moshchnosty Kipyaschiy**, meaning **Large Power Boiling Reactor**.
- The RBMK design had a **positive void coefficient of reactivity**.
  - Simply: when steam bubbles increased in the reactor coolant, reactivity could increase.
  - This could cause power to rise further, creating a dangerous feedback effect.
- Continuous operation below **700 MW(th)** was forbidden.
- Below about **20% power**, around **600 MW(th)**, the reactor had a positive power coefficient.

## Radiation biology

- Ionizing radiation can damage cells by transferring energy to atoms in important biological molecules.
- If damage is not repaired:
  - The cell may die or fail to reproduce.
  - The cell may survive but become modified.
- These outcomes lead to two main types of radiation effects:
  - **Deterministic effects**
  - **Stochastic effects**

## Deterministic effects

- Effects that occur above a **threshold dose**.
- Below the threshold, the likelihood is practically zero.
- Above the threshold, the likelihood and severity increase with dose.
- Example: acute bone marrow syndrome.
- Death is almost certain after a short-term whole-body dose of about **6 Gy or more**.
- Around **3 Gy** may be lethal to half of exposed people with little or no medical care.
- For healthy people with good medical care, **LD50** may be **5 Gy**.
- With very intensive treatment, **LD50** may be as high as **9 Gy**.
- Below **1 Gy**, deterministic effects are practically zero.

## Stochastic effects

- Effects that occur randomly and are statistical in nature.
- Main examples:
  - Cancer in the exposed person.
  - Hereditary effects in later generations.
- The probability of stochastic effects is assumed to increase with dose.
- The source describes the model as **linear, no-threshold**:
  - Any dose, however small, may carry some risk.
  - Risk is roughly proportional to dose.
- Severity of cancer does **not** depend on the dose level.

## International Chernobyl Project

- In **October 1989**, the USSR requested the **International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)** to assess:
  - Whether people could live safely in contaminated areas.
  - Whether protective measures were effective.
- The project assessed affected areas in:
  - Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
  - Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic.
  - Russian Federated Socialist Republic.
- It was formalized in **February 1990** in Moscow.
- Nearly **200 experts** from **25 countries** and **7 multinational organizations** participated.
- Nearly **50 missions** to the USSR took place between **March 1990 and January 1991**.

# Important Facts and Figures

## Accident timeline

- **25 April 1986, 01:00:** Test preparation began by reducing reactor power.
- **25 April, 13:05:** Reactor power reached **50%**, equal to **1,600 MW(th)**.
- **25 April, shortly after 13:05:** Turbogenerator No. 7 was shut down.
- **25 April, shortly after:** Emergency Core Cooling System was isolated.
- Power stayed at this level for about **9 hours**.
- **25 April, 23:00:** Power reduction resumed.
- Power was reduced to **700 MW(th)** but dropped to **30 MW(th)**.
- Power was later raised to **200 MW(th)** by manually withdrawing control rods.
- **26 April, 01:19:** Operators increased feedwater flow and blocked shutdown signals linked to steam drum level and pressure.
- **26 April, 01:23:04:** Reactor trip on loss of the second generator was switched off.
- **26 April, 01:23:30:** Reactor power began to increase.
- **26 April, 01:23:40:** Shift foreman ordered reactor shutdown.
- Within **4 seconds**, power reached about **100 times full power**, around **300,000 MW(th)**.
- Around **01:24:** Explosions occurred.

## Explosion and fire

- About **30% of the fuel** in the core fragmented.
- A **1,000 tonnes core plate** was lifted.
- All fuel channels ruptured.
- About **25% of graphite blocks and fuel channel material** were ejected.
- More than **100 firemen** fought the fires.
- By about **05:00**, all fires except the graphite fire in the core had been extinguished.
- In some areas, radiation levels may have exceeded **100 Gy h⁻¹**.
- Unit 3 was shut down around **03:00**, about **1.5 hours** after the accident.
- Units 1 and 2 were not shut down until the following night, about **24 hours** after the accident.

## Radioactive release

- Unit 4 core inventory at accident time: about **4 × 10¹⁹ Bq**.
- Estimated released volatiles: about **1–2 × 10¹⁸ Bq**.
- Equal amounts of noble gases xenon and krypton were released.
- Noble gases were thought to be completely expelled from the fuel.
- Approximate release fractions:
  - Noble gases: essentially **100%**.
  - Iodine, caesium, tellurium: **10–20%**.
  - Barium, strontium, plutonium, cerium and other refractory radionuclides: **3–6%**.
- Release pattern:
  - Only **25%** occurred during the first day.
  - The rest occurred over a **nine-day period**.
- Activity deposited around Chernobyl:
  - **0.3–0.5%** of core inventory on-site.
  - **1.5–2%** within **20 km**.
  - **1–1.5%** beyond **20 km**.

## Accident management

- Major accident management operation began on **28 April 1986**.
- About **5,000 tonnes** of materials were dropped into the core vault by helicopter.
- Materials included:
  - **Boron carbide (B₄C)** to help prevent recriticality.
  - **Dolomite ((MgCa)(CO₃)₂)** to generate carbon dioxide and help absorb energy from burning graphite.
  - **Clay/sand** to filter radionuclides and quench the fire.
  - **Lead** to absorb heat and form a shielding/sealing layer.

## Evacuations

- **26 April morning:** People in Pripyat were told to remain indoors with windows and doors shut.
- Potassium iodide tablets were distributed from the morning of **26 April**.
- Late on **26 April:** Radiation levels in Pripyat began rising.
- **27 April:** Radiation levels in Pripyat reached around **10 mSv h⁻¹**.
- **27 April noon:** Residents were told to pack provisions for **three days**.
- **27 April, 14:00:** Evacuation of Pripyat began.
- Nearly **1,200 buses** were used.
- Over **44,000 evacuees** left Pripyat.
- The city was emptied in less than **three hours**, except for those with official duties.
- **28 April:** Proposal to evacuate plant site and create a **10 km exclusion zone**.
- **2 May:** Decision to evacuate the “geometric” **30-km zone**.
- **6 May:** Evacuation of the full 30-km zone completed.
- Total evacuated from 30-km zone: **115,000 people**.

## Early health effects inside the USSR

- Acute radiation syndrome diagnosed in **237 persons**.
- Main
